# Week 1: Introduction to Quantum Computing

### Quantum Gates and Circuits - Part 1

- [Music] welcome back to the nptel course on quantum computing basics that's why you've learned about quantum states both mathematically and drawing on ideas from quantum mechanics in this last module of this week's lecture series you will learn how to perform operations on quantum states here's an outline of this module quantum computation is built on certain building blocks called gates and first we will talk about single qubit gates that is how to manipulate single qubits we will discuss quantum circuits using single qubit gates uh then we will move on from single qubit gates to multiple cubic gates or what are called multipartite states and we will uh see how to build uh gates that can operate on multiple qubit states and build more complex circuits uh using those gates before we discuss quantum gauge let's do a quick recap of what you learned earlier this week you learned that in classical computing we have deterministic bits as opposed to uh qubits in quantum computing which are probabilistic uh you learned that quantum computing is built on linear algebra as opposed to the boolean algebra that classical computing is built on and finally uh you learned that bono computing is built on point of gaze as opposed to logic gates of classical computing and it's the last row in this table that we're going to be discussing in depth today a single qubit q uh also uh represented as a get in direct notation is of the form alpha zero plus beta one as you can see here and it's also represented by a uh two row single column matrix [Music] this get is a linear combination of the basis states zero and one which are orthonormal and alpha and beta which are complex numbers are referred to as the probability amplitudes of this qubit the bra which is an operator is the conjugate transpose of a qubit and it is a row matrix as opposed to the get which is a column matrix the bracket is the bra operator applied to the get and that produces a scalar or a complex number and this is also called the inner products just like the vector.product the ket bra is the transformation of one operator into another it transforms a bra into a linear transformation operator or a matrix using the outer product and as you can see the notation for the ketra is the reverse of the bracket you also learned that every qubit when it's observed or when it is measured probabilistically collapses to either of its basis states zero or one and you learned that the probabilities are definite probability of collapsing to zero is the square of the modulus of associated probability amplitude alpha and probability of collapsing to one is the modulus of beta square and because the qubit can only collapse to either zero or the one the sum of those probabilities must add to one uh we learned how to uh represent the qubit or visualize the qubit on the block sphere and we learned that the qubit can be represented in general terms using two angles theta which is the angle between the qubit vector and the z-axis and using phi which is the relative phase of the of the qubit and that's the angle between the qubit and the x-axis or in other words you can also think of it as the cubist rotation around z-axis all right now diving into single qubit gauge a single qubit gate is the most elementary computation you can see in any quantum computing system and its purpose is to change the state of a qubit so take a qubit q and we apply a gate a and it produces another qubit q dash in mathematical terms this is nothing but a linear transformation of a vector so q is a vector or a ket q dash the vector or get as well so a is a matrix and the operation that converts q to q dash is a matrix multiplication and we can describe in general terms any such gate a using a two by two matrix that you see here each element being complex number we can also describe this uh or represent this matrix in dirac form as you can see this is a simply a sum of several

if you take any general qubit cube it's going to simply flip the probability amplitudes so if alpha was associated with zero and beta with one now you'll see beta associate zero and alpha associated with one uh and you if you if you replace one zero with alpha and beta you'll find that the output will be beta alpha so if you do the math you'll find you can verify that so in visual terms and what is if we take the analogy with the classical computing we have uh our autonomous basis states zero and one roughly analogous to the classical zero and one bits zero flips to one and one flips to zero there are several ways of looking at this transformation the poly z gate is uh performs something uh related so this is the matrix and this is the direct form of the poly z gate let's see what it does each gate carries out an elementary computation as you just saw the circuit is supposed to carry out more complex computation building on the gates that it is composed of to take this as example you have an input queue which is a qubit and in the circuit we apply a poly excavate and a hadamard gate in sequence what does this synthesize to the net effect of this uh of applying an x and h gate is multiplied by sigma x and that's what you see here and so any gates that we have in sequence uh to find out what the net transformation is all we have to do is do a matrix multiplication of the gates in the c so in the case of h times sigma x this is the final matrix we get or the final linear transformation and note that any any such combination of gates is also going to produce a unitary matrix just like any single gate does when we apply this particular circuit to get zero if you do the math you'll figure out that the state 0 is going to transform to the state minus whereas the state 1 is going to be transformed to the state plus another example of a circuit we apply a hadamard and an escaped sequence so the matrix is going to be again multiplication the reverse order s times h and this circuit ends up transforming the 0 to a plus i and a 1 to a minus i and visually speaking or if you look at the block sphere this ends up switching the our qubit between the y and the z basis so let's look at the quantum circuit in more general terms so quantum circuit is the purpose of using quantum circuit is to do some use of computation from a given input and then measure the results so we can get something uh interesting out of it so uh as an example look at this particular circuit and note that the timeline goes from left to right so operations happen in this direction first we have an input qubit q we perform some kind of computation this x and h are just an example you can have any sequence of gates here and as a result of the computation we get a different qubit so q changes into q dash in this case uh in this example it'll just be h sigma x uh multiplied by q and that's what uh we'll get now finally as you know uh a qubit when we observe its state it tends to collapse to either of its basic states so that's what this is actually showing you uh the you can see here the uh this line c at the bottom this denotes what the measured state of a qubit is going to be and we know that uh for this cubit q dash if we represent it as alpha dash zero plus beta dash one it's going to collapse to zero with the probability mod alpha dot square and to one with probability mod beta dash square and this this symbol simply denotes that after we perform a computation on uh a qubit via certain sequence of gates we measure that the state of the final superposition state and that's what we get on c and what we end up observing in any one in any real quantum computation system is this measured value c so we see a lot more to do about quantum
